cot_prefix: Okay, let's read the question and the letter carefully to determine the correct answer. The question asks: "According to the letter, the Indians believed that _." The letter is a response to the invitation to send their boys to William and Mary College. The Indians refuse the offer and explain why. The key part of the letter is: "We are sure that you mean to do us good by your proposal ; and we thank you heartily. But you must know that different nations have different ways of looking at things, and you will therefore not be offended if our ideas of this kind of education happen not to be the same as yours." The Indians also mention that they had experience with young people brought up at the colleges of the northern provinces, and when they came back, they were "bad manners, ignorant of every means of living in the woods—they were totally good for nothing." This suggests that the Indians believed that their own way of education was better suited for their young men. They thought that the education offered by the officials (from the college) was not as effective in preparing their young men for their way of life. They also hint that they would be willing to educate the officials' sons in their own way. Let's analyze the options: (A) it would be better for their boys to receive some schooling - This is not what the letter says. They explicitly refused the offer, implying they do NOT think it's better for their boys to receive that schooling. (B) they were being insulted by the offer - The letter is polite and appreciative, thanking the officials and saying they mean to do good. There's no indication of feeling insulted. (C) they knew more about science than the officials - The letter does not mention science. In fact, it's the officials who are offering "your sciences," suggesting that the officials know more about science. (D) they had better way of educating young men - This aligns
